route53DnssecNotEnabled.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 public hosted zone(s) do not have DNSSEC signing enabled (get_dnssec.Status.ServeSignature != 'SIGNING'). Without DNSSEC, resolvers cannot verify the authenticity of DNS responses, which enables cache-poisoning and man-in-the-middle attacks against your domain. DNSSEC is required by many compliance frameworks (e.g., US Federal, PCI-DSS interpretations).
route53DnssecNotEnabled.shortDesc: Enable DNSSEC signing on every public hosted zone

route53QueryLoggingNotEnabled.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 public hosted zone(s) have no DNS query logging configuration (list_query_logging_configs returns empty). Without query logging, there is no CloudWatch Logs record of who resolved what — blocking incident response, threat hunting, and audit. This is Security Hub control Route53.2.
route53QueryLoggingNotEnabled.shortDesc: Enable DNS query logging for every public hosted zone

route53DomainAutoRenewDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53-registered domain(s) have AutoRenew=false. Without automatic renewal the domain will expire on the ExpirationDate; expired domains are auction-listed and can be registered by attackers within days of expiry, enabling full DNS/email takeover.
route53DomainAutoRenewDisabled.shortDesc: Enable AutoRenew on every registered domain

route53DomainTransferLockDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53-registered domain(s) do not have the clientTransferProhibited EPP status set (transfer lock disabled). Without a transfer lock, a compromised registrar credential or successful social-engineering can move the domain to another registrar, giving the attacker full control of DNS and email.
route53DomainTransferLockDisabled.shortDesc: Enable transfer lock (clientTransferProhibited) on every domain

route53DomainPrivacyDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53-registered domain(s) have at least one contact type (Admin, Registrant, or Tech) without privacy protection enabled. Without privacy, WHOIS records expose the contact's name, email, phone, and postal address — feeding phishing, social-engineering, and spam campaigns targeting your organisation.
route53DomainPrivacyDisabled.shortDesc: Enable privacy protection on all contact types

route53DomainExpiringSoon.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53-registered domain(s) will expire within the next 90 days. Even with AutoRenew enabled, payment failures or expired credit cards can still cause loss of the domain; when AutoRenew is disabled the risk is severe. Domains within 30 days of expiry are flagged as HIGH severity.
route53DomainExpiringSoon.shortDesc: Renew domains that are within 90 days of expiry

route53HealthCheckUsingHttp.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 health check(s) probe endpoints over plain HTTP (Type=HTTP or HTTP_STR_MATCH). HTTP health probes transmit host headers and any string-match payload in cleartext, and can be tampered with by an on-path attacker to force spurious failover or hide real outages.
route53HealthCheckUsingHttp.shortDesc: Migrate HTTP health checks to HTTPS

route53HealthCheckNoAlarm.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 endpoint health check(s) (HTTP/HTTPS/TCP) have no associated CloudWatch alarm (CloudWatchAlarmConfiguration is absent). Without an alarm, health-check state transitions (Healthy → Unhealthy) go unnoticed until users complain, defeating the operational value of the health check.
route53HealthCheckNoAlarm.shortDesc: Attach a CloudWatch alarm to every endpoint health check

route53ResolverDnsFirewallNotConfigured.^description: {$COUNT} region(s) have no Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall rule group associations. Without DNS Firewall, VPC-originated DNS queries can freely resolve C2, malware, and data-exfiltration domains — DNS is one of the most common egress channels used by attackers once inside a VPC.
route53ResolverDnsFirewallNotConfigured.shortDesc: Attach a DNS Firewall rule group to every VPC

route53ResolverQueryLoggingNotEnabled.^description: {$COUNT} region(s) have no Route 53 Resolver query logging enabled (either no ResolverQueryLogConfigs exist, or configs exist without any VPC associations). Resolver query logging captures every DNS query made from every VPC — the single most valuable data source for detecting DNS-tunnel exfiltration, C2 beaconing, and lateral movement.
route53ResolverQueryLoggingNotEnabled.shortDesc: Enable Resolver query logging for every VPC

route53ResolverDnssecValidationDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} region(s) have no Route 53 Resolver DNSSEC validation configs with ValidationStatus=ENABLED. Without validation, VPC resolvers will accept unsigned or invalidly-signed responses even for DNSSEC-signed zones, which negates the protection DNSSEC would otherwise provide against cache poisoning.
route53ResolverDnssecValidationDisabled.shortDesc: Enable DNSSEC validation on Route 53 Resolver for each VPC

route53HostedZoneUnused.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 hosted zone(s) contain only the default NS + SOA records and no user-defined records. Empty zones cost $0.50/month each and expose an attack surface (any dangling NS delegation) with no business value. New zones created in the last 24h are excluded from this check.

route53HealthCheckSlowInterval.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 health check(s) use the 30-second (standard) RequestInterval. The 10-second (fast) interval detects failures 3x faster at $1/month per check instead of $0.50 — a small cost premium for meaningfully faster failover on production endpoints. Advisory only; suppress for non-critical checks.
route53HealthCheckSlowInterval.shortDesc: Consider 10-second RequestInterval for production health checks

route53HealthCheckLowFailureThreshold.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 health check(s) have FailureThreshold=1. A threshold of 1 means a single transient failure (packet loss, brief connection error) will flip the check to Unhealthy and trigger failover — causing unnecessary flapping. The default and recommended minimum is 3.
route53HealthCheckLowFailureThreshold.shortDesc: Set FailureThreshold to at least 3 on Route 53 health checks

route53CnameAtZoneApex.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 hosted zone(s) contain a CNAME record at the zone apex (record name equals the zone name). RFC 1034 prohibits CNAME co-existing with the mandatory NS and SOA records at the apex — the configuration breaks email delivery (MX resolution) and often causes intermittent resolution failures. Route 53 alias records solve this without a CNAME.
route53CnameAtZoneApex.shortDesc: Replace apex CNAME with a Route 53 alias record

route53MxWithoutSpfDmarc.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 hosted zone(s) publish MX records for a domain but lack SPF (TXT with v=spf1) or DMARC (TXT at _dmarc.<domain>). Missing email authentication allows attackers to spoof messages 'From:' your domain in phishing campaigns; failure to publish DMARC also blocks the receipt of aggregate abuse reports.
route53MxWithoutSpfDmarc.shortDesc: Publish SPF and DMARC TXT records for every domain with MX

route53HealthCheckDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 health check(s) have HealthCheckConfig.Disabled=true. A disabled health check always reports Healthy — records that use it for failover will never fail over, and the check cost ($0.50-$1/month) continues to accrue. Either re-enable or delete.
route53HealthCheckDisabled.shortDesc: Re-enable or delete disabled health checks

route53RecordNoHealthCheck.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 hosted zone(s) contain records with an explicit routing policy (Failover, Weighted, Latency, MultiValueAnswer, or Geo) but no HealthCheckId. Without a health check the routing policy cannot decide when to fail traffic away from an unhealthy endpoint, so the record continues to answer with dead endpoints indefinitely.
route53RecordNoHealthCheck.shortDesc: Attach a health check to every routing-policy record

route53DanglingDnsRecords.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 hosted zone(s) contain CNAME or alias records pointing at *.cloudfront.net, *.elb.amazonaws.com, *.s3-website-*.amazonaws.com, or *.elasticbeanstalk.com targets that no longer exist in the same account. Dangling references are the entry point for subdomain-takeover attacks: an attacker can register the abandoned target and serve arbitrary content under your domain.
route53DanglingDnsRecords.shortDesc: Delete dangling DNS records or re-provision the target

route53LowTtlOnStableRecords.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 hosted zone(s) contain non-alias records with TTL < 60 seconds and no routing policy. Very-low TTL amplifies query volume (higher $/million cost) and defeats resolver caching; it is only justified for records that must fail over quickly or are actively being cut over.
route53LowTtlOnStableRecords.shortDesc: Raise TTL to ≥ 300s for stable, single-endpoint records

route53PublicZoneSensitiveNames.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 public hosted zone(s) contain records with names such as internal.*, private.*, staging.*, dev.*, vpn.*, admin.*, db.*, or database.* published to the public internet. Public disclosure of internal infrastructure names provides reconnaissance value to an attacker — advisory only; move to a private hosted zone or split-horizon DNS if these names are meant to be internal.
route53PublicZoneSensitiveNames.shortDesc: Move internal-only names to a private hosted zone

route53HealthCheckSniDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 HTTPS health check(s) have EnableSNI=false. Without SNI, TLS servers hosting multiple certificates (CloudFront, ALB with SNI, CDN vendors) cannot select the correct certificate for the probe — the check may validate a wrong or default certificate and produce misleading Healthy results.
route53HealthCheckSniDisabled.shortDesc: Enable SNI on every HTTPS health check

route53EmptyHostedZone.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 hosted zone(s) report ResourceRecordSetCount = 2 (only the mandatory NS + SOA), indicating the zone has never been populated. Fast-path variant of route53HostedZoneUnused using get_hosted_zone.ResourceRecordSetCount instead of a full record listing.

route53NoRecordRoutingPolicy.^description: {$COUNT} Route 53 hosted zone(s) contain A/AAAA records with no routing policy (no Failover, Weight, Region, MultiValueAnswer, or GeoLocation). Records pointing directly at a single IP without a routing policy are single-point-of-failure records — most legitimate deployments front the endpoint with an ELB/CloudFront alias or use a routing policy. Advisory only, high false-positive rate.
route53NoRecordRoutingPolicy.shortDesc: Consider routing policies for redundancy on direct-IP records
